title: Wall Street's AI chip money rotates from Nvidia to Intel, AMD, and Micron
subtitle: After two years of single-stock dominance, a Thursday rally pushed AI infrastructure capital toward second-tier semiconductor names with sharper growth curves.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: On May 8, AI infrastructure stocks rallied across the board, but the leadership shifted. Nvidia is up 15% year-to-date — roughly tracking the Nasdaq — despite revenue growth still expected at 70% this fiscal year. AMD is up 97% year-to-date and 327% over the past year, after Q1 FY2026 revenue hit $10.25 billion (up 38% year over year) and data-center revenue hit $5.78 billion (up 57%). Management guided Q2 to $11.2 billion, an acceleration to roughly 46% growth. Intel had its best month on record in April, more than doubling, and rose another 33% in early May after reports that Apple is in talks with Intel and Samsung to make processors for US devices. Micron is also catching the rotation.

The mechanism here is straightforward: Nvidia's growth is decelerating from triple digits, while AMD's is accelerating, and Intel has gone from left-for-dead to a US-policy beneficiary on domestic chip manufacturing. When the relative growth rates flip, the multiple expansion follows the second-tier names. Bank of America hiked its 2026 chips forecast to $1.3 trillion and named Nvidia, Broadcom, Marvell, and AMD as top drivers — note that Nvidia is now one of four headline names rather than the single name.

Zoom out: this is the first time since the AI buildout began that the market is treating the supply chain as a portfolio rather than a monoculture. Optical networking (Corning's $3B Nvidia deal, Prysmian rallying), HBM memory (Micron), CPUs (Intel, AMD), and accelerators (Nvidia, AMD MI-series) are all being repriced separately. That's healthier price discovery, and it's also what happens when a single supplier's pricing power softens.

Takeaway for learners: the AI build-out is not one trade. The compute layer alone has at least four sub-stacks — accelerators, memory, networking, foundry — and each has different bottlenecks, different geopolitics, and different competitive dynamics. If you're trying to understand where AI value actually accrues, watch which sub-stack has the supply constraint that quarter.

sources.title: Wall Street AI chip love moves from Nvidia to Intel, AMD and Micron — CNBC
